dialogue_history.content: Nursing Agent, could you please provide a summary of the patient's nursing care during this admission, including their activity level, any specific observations regarding their abdominal pain or nausea, and the discharge instructions provided to them regarding PICC line care?

The patient remained alert and independent in her activity while nursing monitored her right upper quadrant pain and managed her nausea with as-needed medication. Nursing care focused on maintaining the left-sided PICC line for IV antibiotic administration and coordinating home services for continued therapy. Discharge instructions emphasized keeping the PICC line site clean and dry and completing scheduled follow-up blood work for monitoring.
REQUEST TEST: Please provide details on the patient's Meropenem dosage, frequency, and why the switch from Ertapenem was necessary based on her history of multi-drug resistant cholangitis.

RESULTS: 1. Cholestyramine 4 gm PO DAILY 2. Escitalopram Oxalate 20 mg PO DAILY 3. HydrOXYzine 50 mg PO QHS:PRN itching 4. Lactulose 15 mL PO BID:PRN confusion 5. Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO DAILY 6. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 7. Omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY 8. Rifaximin 550 mg PO BID 9. Temazepam 15 mg PO QHS:PRN insomnia 10. Ursodiol 300 mg PO TID 11. Meropenem 1000 mg IV Q8H (Dosage: 1000 mg; Frequency: Every 8 hours. Note: Switched from Ertapenem per ID due to recurrence of right upper quadrant pain and fever while on Ertapenem and a history of multi-drug resistant cholangitis; course to end 7 days post-ERCP) 12. Furosemide 40 mg PO BID 13. Ondansetron 4 mg PO BID:PRN nausea 14. Spironolactone 100 mg PO BID

Nursing Agent, could you please provide more details on the patient's mental status and whether any signs of hepatic encephalopathy were observed during her hospitalization, especially considering her history and use of lactulose?

The patient remained alert, oriented, and interactive throughout the stay, with no nursing observations of asterixis or confusion. She successfully managed her bowel movements with PRN lactulose, and her mental status was documented as clear and coherent at discharge.

Medication Agent, the hospital course notes that Furosemide and Spironolactone were held on admission because the patient appeared dry. REQUEST TEST: Please clarify the final plan for these diuretics, including whether they were restarted prior to discharge and if the doses remained the same as her pre-admission regimen.

RESULTS: 1. Cholestyramine 4 gm PO DAILY 2. Escitalopram Oxalate 20 mg PO DAILY 3. HydrOXYzine 50 mg PO QHS:PRN itching 4. Lactulose 15 mL PO BID:PRN confusion 5. Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO DAILY 6. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 7. Omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY 8. Rifaximin 550 mg PO BID 9. Temazepam 15 mg PO QHS:PRN insomnia 10. Ursodiol 300 mg PO TID 11. Meropenem 1000 mg IV Q8H 12. Furosemide 40 mg PO BID (Note: Held on admission due to patient appearing dry; restarted prior to discharge at the same pre-admission dose) 13. Ondansetron 4 mg PO BID:PRN nausea 14. Spironolactone 100 mg PO BID (Note: Held on admission due to patient appearing dry; restarted prior to discharge at the same pre-admission dose)

Nursing Agent, could you describe the patient's pain levels after the ERCP procedure and the removal of the biliary sludge? Did she experience any immediate relief or require increased analgesics post-procedure?

Nursing observations post-ERCP indicate the patient remained comfortable and rested in no acute distress, although she continued to exhibit right upper quadrant tenderness upon palpation. No escalation in pain management was required following the procedure, and she continued to manage nausea with as-needed medication. At discharge, her mental status remained clear and she was ambulatory and independent.

Medication Agent, the EGD revealed an 8 mm ulcer at the gastroesophageal junction. REQUEST TEST: Please confirm if the discharge dosage of Omeprazole 40 mg daily was adjusted for this finding and if any other medications were added for ulcer management or if any changes were made to her ursodiol or cholestyramine doses.

RESULTS: 1. Cholestyramine 4 gm PO DAILY (Note: Continued at pre-admission dose) 2. Escitalopram Oxalate 20 mg PO DAILY 3. HydrOXYzine 50 mg PO QHS:PRN itching 4. Lactulose 15 mL PO BID:PRN confusion 5. Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO DAILY 6. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 7. Omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY (Note: Continued at pre-admission dose; no dosage adjustment or supplemental medications were added for the 8 mm gastroesophageal junction ulcer identified on EGD) 8. Rifaximin 550 mg PO BID 9. Temazepam 15 mg PO QHS:PRN insomnia 10. Ursodiol 300 mg PO TID (Note: Continued at pre-admission dose) 11. Meropenem 1000 mg IV Q8H 12. Furosemide 40 mg PO BID 13. Ondansetron 4 mg PO BID:PRN nausea 14. Spironolactone 100 mg PO BID

Nursing Agent, given the patient's history of Celiac disease and the discovery of an 8 mm ulcer at the gastroesophageal junction during the EGD, could you provide details on the patient's diet during the hospital stay and any specific dietary instructions provided for discharge?

The patient was maintained on a gluten-free diet throughout her stay due to her history of Celiac disease. Nursing monitored her oral intake, which was initially poor due to nausea but improved with as-needed medication. Discharge instructions did not specify new dietary restrictions related to the ulcer, but the patient was advised to follow up with her hepatology team regarding further management.
REQUEST TEST: Please confirm the specific scheduled end date for the Meropenem therapy and verify if any adjustments were made to the Escitalopram or Levothyroxine during the stay.

RESULTS: 1. Cholestyramine 4 gm PO DAILY 2. Escitalopram Oxalate 20 mg PO DAILY (Note: Continued at pre-admission dose; no adjustments were made during the stay) 3. HydrOXYzine 50 mg PO QHS:PRN itching 4. Lactulose 15 mL PO BID:PRN confusion 5. Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO DAILY (Note: Continued at pre-admission dose; no adjustments were made during the stay) 6. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 7. Omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY 8. Rifaximin 550 mg PO BID 9. Temazepam 15 mg PO QHS:PRN insomnia 10. Ursodiol 300 mg PO TID 11. Meropenem 1000 mg IV Q8H (Note: Scheduled to end on ___ , which is 7 days post-ERCP) 12. Furosemide 40 mg PO BID 13. Ondansetron 4 mg PO BID:PRN nausea 14. Spironolactone 100 mg PO BID

Nursing Agent, could you please clarify if the patient was educated on specific signs of recurrent cholangitis to watch for at home, such as a fever threshold or worsening jaundice, and how she tolerated her gluten-free diet by the time of discharge?

The patient was instructed to monitor for a recurrence of fever and was informed that her blood work must be checked next week to monitor her condition. Her tolerance of the gluten-free diet improved during the stay as her nausea was managed, and she was documented as independent and comfortable at discharge.

Medication Agent, the MRCP noted marrow edema in the ascending colon (suggestive of portal colopathy) and ascites. REQUEST TEST: Please confirm if any new medications were initiated for these findings, such as adjustments to the portal hypertension regimen, or if the plan remained focused on the pre-admission medications.

RESULTS: 1. Cholestyramine 4 gm PO DAILY 2. Escitalopram Oxalate 20 mg PO DAILY 3. HydrOXYzine 50 mg PO QHS:PRN itching 4. Lactulose 15 mL PO BID:PRN confusion 5. Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg PO DAILY 6. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 7. Omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY 8. Rifaximin 550 mg PO BID 9. Temazepam 15 mg PO QHS:PRN insomnia 10. Ursodiol 300 mg PO TID 11. Meropenem 1000 mg IV Q8H 12. Furosemide 40 mg PO BID 13. Ondansetron 4 mg PO BID:PRN nausea 14. Spironolactone 100 mg PO BID (Note: No new medications were initiated for the ascites or the marrow edema/portal colopathy findings; management remained focused on pre-admission medications, including restarting diuretics. Nadolol was not initiated due to a known allergy.)

SUMMARY READY: This 53-year-old female with primary sclerosing cholangitis and cirrhosis (Child-Pugh B, MELD 11) was admitted for right upper quadrant abdominal pain and fever concerning for recurrent cholangitis. Diagnostic imaging with MRCP and ultrasound demonstrated biliary sludge and a stone in the gallbladder remnant, which was addressed via an ERCP that removed scant sludge from the biliary tree. Given her history of multi-drug resistant infections, her antibiotic therapy was transitioned to Meropenem 1g every 8 hours via a PICC line, to be completed seven days post-procedure. An EGD performed during the admission identified an 8 mm gastroesophageal junction ulcer and portal gastropathy without evidence of active varices. Throughout the hospitalization, the patient remained neurologically intact with no signs of hepatic encephalopathy, and her chronic medications—including rifaximin, lactulose, and ursodiol—were continued. Her home diuretics were held on admission but restarted prior to discharge once her fluid status was stabilized. At the time of discharge, the patient was afebrile, ambulatory, and independent. She was instructed to complete her outpatient IV antibiotic course, continue a gluten-free diet, and follow up for scheduled laboratory monitoring and hepatology clinic appointments.
